content:
AUDIENCE:  
The reader is an adult with a skeptical mindset, likely someone who is cautious about new scientific claims and wary of hype, especially around topics like mRNA due to recent high-profile discussions (e.g., vaccines). They have a basic understanding of biology—perhaps they know DNA stores genetic information but are unsure about how cells actually use that information. They care about practical, clear explanations that avoid jargon and exaggeration. They might get bored or frustrated by overly technical details or abstract concepts without real-world grounding. A straightforward, respectful tone that acknowledges their skepticism and offers clear analogies or simple facts will work best.

===BEGIN===  
mRNA, or messenger RNA, is like a simple instruction manual inside your cells. Think of your DNA as a big library storing all the recipes your body needs to make proteins, which do most of the work in cells. But DNA never leaves the library (the cell’s nucleus). Instead, it sends out copies of specific recipes in the form of mRNA. This mRNA travels to a part of the cell called the ribosome, which reads the instructions and builds the protein step by step. So, mRNA’s job is straightforward: it carries the exact instructions from the DNA to where the proteins are made. This system has been understood for decades and is not some new, untested idea. It’s how cells naturally operate, making mRNA a reliable way to deliver instructions when we want to help the body produce a specific protein.  
===END===
